title: Endometriosis
oneLiner: Ectopic endometrial tissue causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility; affects ~10% of reproductive-age women; diagnosis delayed 7-10 years.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 29-year-old nulliparous woman presents with progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain that began in her teens. The pain is bilateral lower abdominal, cramping, starts 2 days before menstruation and persists through menses. She also reports deep dyspareunia, pain with bowel movements during periods, and has been trying to conceive for 18 months without success. Examination reveals a fixed, retroverted uterus and tender nodularity in the pouch of Douglas.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **Progressive dysmenorrhea since teens** → Suggests organic pathology (not primary dysmenorrhea which typically improves with age/parity)
- **Cyclical pain** → Ectopic endometrial tissue responds to hormonal fluctuations, bleeding into surrounding tissue → inflammation
- **Deep dyspareunia** → Cul-de-sac involvement, uterosacral ligament infiltration → pain on cervical motion/deep penetration
- **Pain with defecation during menses** → Rectovaginal septum or posterior cul-de-sac implants
- **Infertility** → Adhesions distort tubo-ovarian anatomy, inflammatory microenvironment impairs oocyte quality, implantation
- **Fixed retroverted uterus + tender nodularity on rectovaginal exam** → Advanced disease with extensive adhesions and deep infiltrating endometriosis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Asymptomatic endometrioma** – Incidental finding on ultrasound in a patient with no pain. Clue: "chocolate cyst" on imaging, but don't assume benign – risk of rupture or malignant transformation.
- **Cyclical hematuria or rectal bleeding** – Bladder or bowel deep infiltrating endometriosis. Mistaken for UTI or IBS. Clue: symptoms exclusively perimenstrual.
- **Catamenial pneumothorax** – Thoracic endometriosis. Most common cause of recurrent pneumothorax in premenopausal women. Clue: right-sided, associated with menstruation.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial-like glands and stroma outside the uterine cavity, most commonly on the ovaries, pelvic peritoneum, uterosacral ligaments, and rectovaginal septum. The ectopic tissue bleeds cyclically → inflammation, fibrosis, adhesions, and cysts.

**Why it matters:**
- **Prevalence:** 10% of women of reproductive age; up to 50% of women with infertility.
- **Diagnostic delay:** Average 7–10 years – patients are often dismissed as having "period pain" or IBS.
- **Impact:** Chronic pain, infertility, loss of work days, high healthcare utilization. Up to 40% have associated adenomyosis or fibroids.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis as pelvic inflammatory disease or irritable bowel syndrome; failure to perform bimanual and rectovaginal exam; sending patients away with NSAIDs alone without further workup.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** Sampson's theory (retrograde menstruation) is most widely accepted: menstrual debris flows backward through fallopian tubes into the pelvic cavity. This is universal (90% of women have retrograde flow), but only those with immune dysfunction fail to clear the debris → implantation and growth. Additional factors: coelomic metaplasia (peritoneal cells transform into endometrial tissue) and lymphatic/hematogenous spread.

**Mechanism → Clinical signs:**
- **Ectopic endometrium bleeds cyclically** → Released iron, prostaglandins, cytokines cause local inflammation → Pain (dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia)
- **Inflammation → adhesion formation** → Fixes pelvic organs → Fixed retroverted uterus, reduced ovarian mobility, obliterated pouch of Douglas
- **Endometriomas** – Ovarian invagination of ectopic endometrium → "Chocolate cysts" (old blood). Can grow large, rupture, or undergo torsion.
- **Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE)** – Nodules >5 mm invading >5 mm into peritoneum, often in uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, bowel, bladder → Deep pain, dyschezia, dysuria
- **Infertility** – Multifactorial: adhesions block tubes, inflammatory peritoneal fluid impairs sperm/oocyte function, altered endometrial receptivity

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- Cyclical pain → bleeds with menses
- Deep dyspareunia → uterosacral ligament involvement stretched during intercourse
- Fixed uterus → widespread adhesions
- Infertility with normal tubes by HSG → inflammatory factor
- Pain at defecation → rectovaginal or bowel implants

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause/Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most common – no clear trigger | "Woman with no known risk factors" |
| **Genetic** | First-degree relative with endometriosis | "Mother or sister had it" |
| **Anatomical** | Obstructive Müllerian anomalies (e.g., non-communicating rudimentary horn) | "Cyclical pain since menarche with pelvic mass" |
| **Iatrogenic** | Cesarean section scar, episiotomy scar | "Cyclical pain and swelling at incision site" |
| **Environmental** | Dioxin exposure (controversial, but supported by primate studies) | Rare in vignettes; more historical interest |
| **Immunological** | Defective clearance of retrograde menstrual debris | "Persistent inflammation despite normal WCC" |
| **Hormonal** | Early menarche, short cycles, nulliparity, low BMI | Classic risk factors – but often present in stem without mention |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, with genetic predisposition and retrograde menstruation as necessary condition.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Gold standard is **laparoscopic visualisation** with histologic confirmation. Empiric diagnosis can be made clinically (typical symptoms + imaging findings) to start treatment, but definite diagnosis requires surgery. Staging: **rASRM (revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine)** score – I (minimal) to IV (severe). However, stage does not correlate well with pain.

**Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Transvaginal ultrasound** | Ovarian endometriomas (ground-glass echogenicity, no internal vascularity), reduced ovarian mobility, "kissing ovaries" sign | First-line imaging | Endometrioma: round, homogenous, low-level echoes, well-defined wall. Absence of endometrioma does NOT rule out peritoneal disease. "Soft markers": fixed ovary, site-specific tenderness |
| **MRI pelvis** | Deep infiltrating endometriosis (nodules, fibrosis, bright signal on T1 with shading on T2), bowel/ bladder involvement | Suspected DIE, before complex surgery | "T1-bright, T2-shading" for endometriomas. Obliteration of fat plane between uterus and rectum = DIE |
| **Laparoscopy** | Direct visualisation of implants, adhesions, endometriomas | Definitive diagnosis and treatment | "Powder-burn" lesions (black/puckered), red/pink vesicles (active), clear blebs, white scarring. Biopsy confirms endometrial glands and stroma |
| **CA-125** | Elevated in moderate-severe endometriosis | Not diagnostic – used as adjunct pre-surgery | >35 U/mL suggests advanced disease but low specificity (also elevated in ovarian cancer, PID, adenomyosis) |
| **Diagnostic hysteroscopy + biopsy** | Uterine cavity assessment | If abnormal uterine bleeding or suspected adenomyosis | Not routine for endometriosis |

**Single finding that confirms:** Laparoscopic identification of endometrial implants. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal laparoscopy in symptomatic patient – ~10-20% have false negative (superficial lesions missed). Negative laparoscopy with persistent symptoms may need repeat by expert.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – acute pain exacerbation:**
- **NSAIDs:** Ibuprofen 600 mg PO stat or Naproxen 500 mg PO, then 250-500 mg BD (courses max 5 days) → Blocks prostaglandins
- **Paracetamol:** 1 g PO/IV q6h as adjunct → Central analgesia
- **Avoid opioids** – high recurrence, tolerance, without treating underlying inflammation. Use only if severe breakthrough.
- If acute abdomen (rigidity, guarding, rebound, fever) → nil by mouth, IV fluids, urgent surgical review (ruptured endometrioma, torsion).

**Ward (24-72h) – hormonal suppression for chronic management:**
- **Combined oral contraceptive (COC):** Continuous (tricycling) – e.g., ethinylestradiol 20 mcg + desogestrel 150 mcg daily for 12 weeks, then 1-week break → Decidualization of ectopic endometrium, anovulation
- **Progestogen-only:** Norethisterone 10-15 mg PO daily continuously or Dienogest 2 mg PO daily → More potent suppression, less estrogenic side effects. Dienogest is first-line in many guidelines.
- **GnRH agonists:** Goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly or Leuprolide 3.75 mg IM monthly – induce medical menopause (add-back HRT to prevent bone loss if >6 months)
- **Levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena):** 52 mg intrauterine – effective for menstrual pain, but less for deep dyspareunia
- **Surgery:** Laparoscopic excision/fulguration of endometriotic lesions + adhesiolysis, ovarian cystectomy for endometriomas. For fertility: conservative surgery first. For pain recurrence – hormonal suppression post-op.

**Long-term (months-years):**
- **Fertility:** If trying to conceive → no suppression. Consider IVF if stage III-IV. Optimize ovarian reserve before surgery.
- **Pain recurrence prevention:** Continuous COC or progestogen after surgery. Dienogest as maintenance.
- **Multidisciplinary:** Pain psychologist, pelvic floor physiotherapy, dietary approaches (anti-inflammatory, low FODMAP if IBS overlap)
- **Surgical reconsideration:** Repeat surgery only if severe recurrence not controlled medically – more adhesions each time.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT rely solely on analgesics without hormonal treatment – progression of disease
- Do NOT use GnRH agonists without add-back therapy for >6 months (bone density loss)
- Do NOT perform simple drainage of endometrioma without cyst wall excision – high recurrence
- Do NOT attempt IVF without first addressing large endometriomas (>3 cm) – risk of infection/rupture
- Do NOT attribute all pelvic pain to endometriosis after negative laparoscopy – consider pelvic congestion, nerve entrapment, adenomyosis

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Endometriosis | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease | Ovarian Cyst (simple/hemorrhagic) | Irritable Bowel Syndrome | Adenomyosis |
|---------|---------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Cyclical, worsening perimenstrually, deep dyspareunia, pain with defecation | Constant low pelvic pain, worsened by movement, cervical motion tenderness | Acute onset if rupture/torsion; constant dull ache if large | Crampy lower abdominal pain with bloating, relieved with defecation; not necessarily cyclical | Heavy menstrual pain, but also non-cyclical pelvic pressure; dyspareunia |
| **Exam** | Fixed retroverted uterus, uterosacral nodularity, tender Pouch of Douglas | Cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, purulent discharge if active | Mobile adnexal mass without nodularity; may be tender if torsion/rupture | Benign abdominal exam, no mass or cervical motion tenderness | Enlarged, boggy uterus (≤12 wk size); diffuse tenderness |
| **Imaging** | TVUS: endometriomas (ground-glass), "kissing ovaries" on adhesion; MRI: T1 bright, T2 shading for DIE | TVUS: thickened fallopian tubes with incomplete septations ("cogwheel sign"), free fluid; occasionally tubo-ovarian abscess | TVUS: simple cyst (anechoic, thin wall) or hemorrhagic (reticular pattern, no flow) | Normal imaging (rule out organic cause) | TVUS: asymmetric myometrial thickening, "glove-finger" sign; MRI: junctional zone thickening >12 mm |
| **Labs** | CA-125 mildly elevated; normal WCC/CRP (unless acute rupture) | Elevated WCC, CRP, positive chlamydia/gonorrhea PCR | Normal WCC; if ruptured – peritoneal signs, may have mild CRP | Normal; stool calprotectin normal to exclude IBD | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Cyclical nature, uterosacral nodularity, infertility | Positive cervical swab, fever, raised inflammatory markers | Acute onset with anechoic/hemorrhagic cyst on imaging | Bloating + relief with defecation, no pelvic tenderness | Heavy menstrual bleeding + enlarged boggy uterus without adnexal mass |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications:**

1. **Ovarian endometrioma rupture** → Acute pelvic pain, peritonitis, fever, hypotension (if large bleed). Presents like ruptured ectopic or appendicitis. **Key signs:** sudden severe pain, guarding, referred shoulder tip (rare). **What to do:** IV fluids, urgent surgical referral (diagnostic laparoscopy + cystectomy or oophorectomy if necrotic).

2. **Bowel obstruction from DIE** → Subacute obstructive symptoms that worsen cyclically. Presents with colicky pain, distension, vomiting, absolute constipation during menses, then resolves. **Key signs:** perimenstrual bowel symptoms, rectovaginal nodule on exam. **What to do:** CT abdomen with oral contrast, surgical consult – segmental bowel resection may be needed.

3. **Ureteral obstruction / hydronephrosis** → Silent or flank pain, recurrent UTIs, hypertension. **Key signs:** loss of renal function on USS, high-grade obstruction. **What to do:** Urgent urology referral (ureterolysis, stent placement). Can lead to permanent renal damage if missed.

4. **Infertility** – Most common complication. Tubal occlusion, peritubal adhesions, poor oocyte quality. **Late presentation:** Woman in her late 30s with endometriosis who deferred childbearing → both age and disease reduce success. **What to do:** Early fertility preservation (egg freezing) if anticipated, early referral to reproductive endocrinology.

5. **Malignant transformation** – Rare (1% of endometriomas → clear cell or endometrioid ovarian carcinoma). **Clue:** endometrioma that grows despite menopause or on continuous hormonal suppression, new solid component or vascular flow on US. **What to do:** MRI + tumor markers (CA-125, HE4), gynecologic oncology consult.

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- **"30-year-old woman with chronic pelvic pain and infertility for 5 years"** – underscores delay: early diagnosis could have prevented adhesions and preserved fertility.
- **"Postmenopausal woman with new pelvic mass"** – suspect malignant transformation in pre-existing endometriosis if she had symptoms previously.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **A-B-C-D-E of endometriosis presentation:**
  - **A** – Adhesions (fixed uterus, "kissing ovaries")
  - **B** – Bleeding (cyclical, at implant sites)
  - **C** – Cysts (endometriomas, "chocolate cysts")
  - **D** – Dyspareunia (deep), Dyschezia (painful defecation)
  - **E** – Endometriomas, Ectopic tissue everywhere

- **"S-E-N-S-I-T-I-V-E"** for exam findings:  
  - **S** – Scar tenderness (if previous C-section/episiotomy)
  - **E** – Endometrioma on US
  - **N** – Nodularity on rectovaginal exam
  - **S** – Site-specific tenderness (e.g., uterosacral)
  - **I** – Infertility history
  - **T** – Tenderness on cervical motion
  - **I** – Immobile uterus
  - **V** – Vaginal/rectal bleeding with menses
  - **E** – Elevated CA-125

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Deep dyspareunia is the single most specific symptom for DIE** – ask every woman with pelvic pain. Pain with defecation during menses is equally specific.
2. **If you can feel it, it's advanced disease.** Nodularity on exam usually means stage III-IV. Peritoneal only (stage I) is often not palpable.
3. **Normal ultrasound does not rule out endometriosis.** Superficial peritoneal implants are invisible. Only a negative laparoscopy by an expert surgeon rules it out.
4. **Endometriomas can mimic ovarian cancer** – but ground-glass echoes, no solid vascular component, and CA-125 <200 point to benignity. New solid areas are suspicious.
5. **A patient with endometriosis and acute abdomen needs urgent surgical evaluation** – consider ruptured endometrioma, torsion of dermoid with endometrioma, or tubo-ovarian abscess.
6. **Dienogest is the medical mainstay for long-term pain management** – it is a fourth-generation progestin with high endometrial selectivity and low metabolic side effects. Cost limits use in some settings.
7. **Always consider co-existing adenomyosis** – heavy bleeding with pain suggests adenomyosis; endometriosis alone often has severe pain but normal flow. MRI or TVUS pre-treatment helps.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Sending patient home with NSAIDs and no referral → delays diagnosis by years
- Performing laparoscopy and not biopsying suspicious lesions → missed diagnosis
- Assuming negative laparoscopy means no endometriosis (false negative up to 20%)
- Starting GnRH agonist without add-back for >6 months → irreversible bone loss
- Recommending hysterectomy alone (if ovaries left) → recurrence risk high (ovarian hormone still stimulates remaining implants)
- Treating infertility with ART without first surgically treating large endometriomas → risk of infection/rupture during stimulation

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior/on-call team:**

| Scenario | Threshold / Signs | Action |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Severe acute pain unresponsive to oral analgesics** | VAS ≥8, unable to tolerate oral, need IV analgesia | Call gynecology registrar – consider laparoscopy for suspected rupture/torsion |
| **Peritoneal signs** | Rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds | Nil by mouth, IV fluids, urgent surgical evaluation (ruptured endometrioma, bowel perforation from DIE, torsion) |
| **Sepsis suspected** | Fever >38°C, tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate | NEWS2 ≥5, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, urgent imaging – possible tubo-ovarian abscess |
| **Hemodynamic instability** | SBP <90, HR >120, lactate >2 mmol/L | IV fluids, cross-match, call OBGYN reg ± general surgery reg (hemorrhage from ruptured cyst) |
| **Reduced urine output / flank pain** | Anuria, oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr, flank tenderness | Urgent renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis), urology referral |
| **GCS drop / seizure / severe headache** | Epidural or intracranial manifestation – rare (catamenial) | Call ICU, neurology – image brain for subarachnoid hemorrhage or epilepsy |
| **Suspected malignant transformation** | Solid component in endometrioma, rising CA-125 >200, family history of BRCA, postmenopausal | Gynaecologic oncology consult, MRI with contrast, tumor markers |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** triggers urgent clinical review
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with shock → ICU consult
- **GCS ≤14** in a patient on GnRH or hormonal therapy – rule out stroke/intracranial involvement
- **Serum sodium <125 mmol/L** if on GnRH agonist with mania/hyponatremia (SIADH-like effect reported rarely)

**Bottom line:** Endometriosis is a complex, chronic disease that requires a high index of suspicion, careful clinical exam, and a multidisciplinary approach. Your job as an FY1 is to recognize the pattern, start hormonal suppression early, and escalate promptly for acute complications. Always ask about pain with periods, deep pain during sex, and fertility goals. You can change a patient’s trajectory by diagnosing early.

rawMarkdown:
# Endometriosis

**One-liner:** Ectopic endometrial tissue causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility; affects ~10% of reproductive-age women; diagnosis delayed 7-10 years.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 29-year-old nulliparous woman presents with progressively worsening cyclical pelvic pain that began in her teens. The pain is bilateral lower abdominal, cramping, starts 2 days before menstruation and persists through menses. She also reports deep dyspareunia, pain with bowel movements during periods, and has been trying to conceive for 18 months without success. Examination reveals a fixed, retroverted uterus and tender nodularity in the pouch of Douglas.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **Progressive dysmenorrhea since teens** → Suggests organic pathology (not primary dysmenorrhea which typically improves with age/parity)
- **Cyclical pain** → Ectopic endometrial tissue responds to hormonal fluctuations, bleeding into surrounding tissue → inflammation
- **Deep dyspareunia** → Cul-de-sac involvement, uterosacral ligament infiltration → pain on cervical motion/deep penetration
- **Pain with defecation during menses** → Rectovaginal septum or posterior cul-de-sac implants
- **Infertility** → Adhesions distort tubo-ovarian anatomy, inflammatory microenvironment impairs oocyte quality, implantation
- **Fixed retroverted uterus + tender nodularity on rectovaginal exam** → Advanced disease with extensive adhesions and deep infiltrating endometriosis

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Asymptomatic endometrioma** – Incidental finding on ultrasound in a patient with no pain. Clue: "chocolate cyst" on imaging, but don't assume benign – risk of rupture or malignant transformation.
- **Cyclical hematuria or rectal bleeding** – Bladder or bowel deep infiltrating endometriosis. Mistaken for UTI or IBS. Clue: symptoms exclusively perimenstrual.
- **Catamenial pneumothorax** – Thoracic endometriosis. Most common cause of recurrent pneumothorax in premenopausal women. Clue: right-sided, associated with menstruation.

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial-like glands and stroma outside the uterine cavity, most commonly on the ovaries, pelvic peritoneum, uterosacral ligaments, and rectovaginal septum. The ectopic tissue bleeds cyclically → inflammation, fibrosis, adhesions, and cysts.

**Why it matters:**
- **Prevalence:** 10% of women of reproductive age; up to 50% of women with infertility.
- **Diagnostic delay:** Average 7–10 years – patients are often dismissed as having "period pain" or IBS.
- **Impact:** Chronic pain, infertility, loss of work days, high healthcare utilization. Up to 40% have associated adenomyosis or fibroids.
- **Common pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis as pelvic inflammatory disease or irritable bowel syndrome; failure to perform bimanual and rectovaginal exam; sending patients away with NSAIDs alone without further workup.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:** Sampson's theory (retrograde menstruation) is most widely accepted: menstrual debris flows backward through fallopian tubes into the pelvic cavity. This is universal (90% of women have retrograde flow), but only those with immune dysfunction fail to clear the debris → implantation and growth. Additional factors: coelomic metaplasia (peritoneal cells transform into endometrial tissue) and lymphatic/hematogenous spread.

**Mechanism → Clinical signs:**
- **Ectopic endometrium bleeds cyclically** → Released iron, prostaglandins, cytokines cause local inflammation → Pain (dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia)
- **Inflammation → adhesion formation** → Fixes pelvic organs → Fixed retroverted uterus, reduced ovarian mobility, obliterated pouch of Douglas
- **Endometriomas** – Ovarian invagination of ectopic endometrium → "Chocolate cysts" (old blood). Can grow large, rupture, or undergo torsion.
- **Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE)** – Nodules >5 mm invading >5 mm into peritoneum, often in uterosacral ligaments, rectovaginal septum, bowel, bladder → Deep pain, dyschezia, dysuria
- **Infertility** – Multifactorial: adhesions block tubes, inflammatory peritoneal fluid impairs sperm/oocyte function, altered endometrial receptivity

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- Cyclical pain → bleeds with menses
- Deep dyspareunia → uterosacral ligament involvement stretched during intercourse
- Fixed uterus → widespread adhesions
- Infertility with normal tubes by HSG → inflammatory factor
- Pain at defecation → rectovaginal or bowel implants

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause/Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most common – no clear trigger | "Woman with no known risk factors" |
| **Genetic** | First-degree relative with endometriosis | "Mother or sister had it" |
| **Anatomical** | Obstructive Müllerian anomalies (e.g., non-communicating rudimentary horn) | "Cyclical pain since menarche with pelvic mass" |
| **Iatrogenic** | Cesarean section scar, episiotomy scar | "Cyclical pain and swelling at incision site" |
| **Environmental** | Dioxin exposure (controversial, but supported by primate studies) | Rare in vignettes; more historical interest |
| **Immunological** | Defective clearance of retrograde menstrual debris | "Persistent inflammation despite normal WCC" |
| **Hormonal** | Early menarche, short cycles, nulliparity, low BMI | Classic risk factors – but often present in stem without mention |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, with genetic predisposition and retrograde menstruation as necessary condition.

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Gold standard is **laparoscopic visualisation** with histologic confirmation. Empiric diagnosis can be made clinically (typical symptoms + imaging findings) to start treatment, but definite diagnosis requires surgery. Staging: **rASRM (revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine)** score – I (minimal) to IV (severe). However, stage does not correlate well with pain.

**Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Transvaginal ultrasound** | Ovarian endometriomas (ground-glass echogenicity, no internal vascularity), reduced ovarian mobility, "kissing ovaries" sign | First-line imaging | Endometrioma: round, homogenous, low-level echoes, well-defined wall. Absence of endometrioma does NOT rule out peritoneal disease. "Soft markers": fixed ovary, site-specific tenderness |
| **MRI pelvis** | Deep infiltrating endometriosis (nodules, fibrosis, bright signal on T1 with shading on T2), bowel/ bladder involvement | Suspected DIE, before complex surgery | "T1-bright, T2-shading" for endometriomas. Obliteration of fat plane between uterus and rectum = DIE |
| **Laparoscopy** | Direct visualisation of implants, adhesions, endometriomas | Definitive diagnosis and treatment | "Powder-burn" lesions (black/puckered), red/pink vesicles (active), clear blebs, white scarring. Biopsy confirms endometrial glands and stroma |
| **CA-125** | Elevated in moderate-severe endometriosis | Not diagnostic – used as adjunct pre-surgery | >35 U/mL suggests advanced disease but low specificity (also elevated in ovarian cancer, PID, adenomyosis) |
| **Diagnostic hysteroscopy + biopsy** | Uterine cavity assessment | If abnormal uterine bleeding or suspected adenomyosis | Not routine for endometriosis |

**Single finding that confirms:** Laparoscopic identification of endometrial implants. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal laparoscopy in symptomatic patient – ~10-20% have false negative (superficial lesions missed). Negative laparoscopy with persistent symptoms may need repeat by expert.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – acute pain exacerbation:**
- **NSAIDs:** Ibuprofen 600 mg PO stat or Naproxen 500 mg PO, then 250-500 mg BD (courses max 5 days) → Blocks prostaglandins
- **Paracetamol:** 1 g PO/IV q6h as adjunct → Central analgesia
- **Avoid opioids** – high recurrence, tolerance, without treating underlying inflammation. Use only if severe breakthrough.
- If acute abdomen (rigidity, guarding, rebound, fever) → nil by mouth, IV fluids, urgent surgical review (ruptured endometrioma, torsion).

**Ward (24-72h) – hormonal suppression for chronic management:**
- **Combined oral contraceptive (COC):** Continuous (tricycling) – e.g., ethinylestradiol 20 mcg + desogestrel 150 mcg daily for 12 weeks, then 1-week break → Decidualization of ectopic endometrium, anovulation
- **Progestogen-only:** Norethisterone 10-15 mg PO daily continuously or Dienogest 2 mg PO daily → More potent suppression, less estrogenic side effects. Dienogest is first-line in many guidelines.
- **GnRH agonists:** Goserelin 3.6 mg SC monthly or Leuprolide 3.75 mg IM monthly – induce medical menopause (add-back HRT to prevent bone loss if >6 months)
- **Levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena):** 52 mg intrauterine – effective for menstrual pain, but less for deep dyspareunia
- **Surgery:** Laparoscopic excision/fulguration of endometriotic lesions + adhesiolysis, ovarian cystectomy for endometriomas. For fertility: conservative surgery first. For pain recurrence – hormonal suppression post-op.

**Long-term (months-years):**
- **Fertility:** If trying to conceive → no suppression. Consider IVF if stage III-IV. Optimize ovarian reserve before surgery.
- **Pain recurrence prevention:** Continuous COC or progestogen after surgery. Dienogest as maintenance.
- **Multidisciplinary:** Pain psychologist, pelvic floor physiotherapy, dietary approaches (anti-inflammatory, low FODMAP if IBS overlap)
- **Surgical reconsideration:** Repeat surgery only if severe recurrence not controlled medically – more adhesions each time.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT rely solely on analgesics without hormonal treatment – progression of disease
- Do NOT use GnRH agonists without add-back therapy for >6 months (bone density loss)
- Do NOT perform simple drainage of endometrioma without cyst wall excision – high recurrence
- Do NOT attempt IVF without first addressing large endometriomas (>3 cm) – risk of infection/rupture
- Do NOT attribute all pelvic pain to endometriosis after negative laparoscopy – consider pelvic congestion, nerve entrapment, adenomyosis

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Endometriosis | Pelvic Inflammatory Disease | Ovarian Cyst (simple/hemorrhagic) | Irritable Bowel Syndrome | Adenomyosis |
|---------|---------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Cyclical, worsening perimenstrually, deep dyspareunia, pain with defecation | Constant low pelvic pain, worsened by movement, cervical motion tenderness | Acute onset if rupture/torsion; constant dull ache if large | Crampy lower abdominal pain with bloating, relieved with defecation; not necessarily cyclical | Heavy menstrual pain, but also non-cyclical pelvic pressure; dyspareunia |
| **Exam** | Fixed retroverted uterus, uterosacral nodularity, tender Pouch of Douglas | Cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, purulent discharge if active | Mobile adnexal mass without nodularity; may be tender if torsion/rupture | Benign abdominal exam, no mass or cervical motion tenderness | Enlarged, boggy uterus (≤12 wk size); diffuse tenderness |
| **Imaging** | TVUS: endometriomas (ground-glass), "kissing ovaries" on adhesion; MRI: T1 bright, T2 shading for DIE | TVUS: thickened fallopian tubes with incomplete septations ("cogwheel sign"), free fluid; occasionally tubo-ovarian abscess | TVUS: simple cyst (anechoic, thin wall) or hemorrhagic (reticular pattern, no flow) | Normal imaging (rule out organic cause) | TVUS: asymmetric myometrial thickening, "glove-finger" sign; MRI: junctional zone thickening >12 mm |
| **Labs** | CA-125 mildly elevated; normal WCC/CRP (unless acute rupture) | Elevated WCC, CRP, positive chlamydia/gonorrhea PCR | Normal WCC; if ruptured – peritoneal signs, may have mild CRP | Normal; stool calprotectin normal to exclude IBD | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Cyclical nature, uterosacral nodularity, infertility | Positive cervical swab, fever, raised inflammatory markers | Acute onset with anechoic/hemorrhagic cyst on imaging | Bloating + relief with defecation, no pelvic tenderness | Heavy menstrual bleeding + enlarged boggy uterus without adnexal mass |

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications:**

1. **Ovarian endometrioma rupture** → Acute pelvic pain, peritonitis, fever, hypotension (if large bleed). Presents like ruptured ectopic or appendicitis. **Key signs:** sudden severe pain, guarding, referred shoulder tip (rare). **What to do:** IV fluids, urgent surgical referral (diagnostic laparoscopy + cystectomy or oophorectomy if necrotic).

2. **Bowel obstruction from DIE** → Subacute obstructive symptoms that worsen cyclically. Presents with colicky pain, distension, vomiting, absolute constipation during menses, then resolves. **Key signs:** perimenstrual bowel symptoms, rectovaginal nodule on exam. **What to do:** CT abdomen with oral contrast, surgical consult – segmental bowel resection may be needed.

3. **Ureteral obstruction / hydronephrosis** → Silent or flank pain, recurrent UTIs, hypertension. **Key signs:** loss of renal function on USS, high-grade obstruction. **What to do:** Urgent urology referral (ureterolysis, stent placement). Can lead to permanent renal damage if missed.

4. **Infertility** – Most common complication. Tubal occlusion, peritubal adhesions, poor oocyte quality. **Late presentation:** Woman in her late 30s with endometriosis who deferred childbearing → both age and disease reduce success. **What to do:** Early fertility preservation (egg freezing) if anticipated, early referral to reproductive endocrinology.

5. **Malignant transformation** – Rare (1% of endometriomas → clear cell or endometrioid ovarian carcinoma). **Clue:** endometrioma that grows despite menopause or on continuous hormonal suppression, new solid component or vascular flow on US. **What to do:** MRI + tumor markers (CA-125, HE4), gynecologic oncology consult.

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- **"30-year-old woman with chronic pelvic pain and infertility for 5 years"** – underscores delay: early diagnosis could have prevented adhesions and preserved fertility.
- **"Postmenopausal woman with new pelvic mass"** – suspect malignant transformation in pre-existing endometriosis if she had symptoms previously.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **A-B-C-D-E of endometriosis presentation:**
  - **A** – Adhesions (fixed uterus, "kissing ovaries")
  - **B** – Bleeding (cyclical, at implant sites)
  - **C** – Cysts (endometriomas, "chocolate cysts")
  - **D** – Dyspareunia (deep), Dyschezia (painful defecation)
  - **E** – Endometriomas, Ectopic tissue everywhere

- **"S-E-N-S-I-T-I-V-E"** for exam findings:  
  - **S** – Scar tenderness (if previous C-section/episiotomy)
  - **E** – Endometrioma on US
  - **N** – Nodularity on rectovaginal exam
  - **S** – Site-specific tenderness (e.g., uterosacral)
  - **I** – Infertility history
  - **T** – Tenderness on cervical motion
  - **I** – Immobile uterus
  - **V** – Vaginal/rectal bleeding with menses
  - **E** – Elevated CA-125

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Deep dyspareunia is the single most specific symptom for DIE** – ask every woman with pelvic pain. Pain with defecation during menses is equally specific.
2. **If you can feel it, it's advanced disease.** Nodularity on exam usually means stage III-IV. Peritoneal only (stage I) is often not palpable.
3. **Normal ultrasound does not rule out endometriosis.** Superficial peritoneal implants are invisible. Only a negative laparoscopy by an expert surgeon rules it out.
4. **Endometriomas can mimic ovarian cancer** – but ground-glass echoes, no solid vascular component, and CA-125 <200 point to benignity. New solid areas are suspicious.
5. **A patient with endometriosis and acute abdomen needs urgent surgical evaluation** – consider ruptured endometrioma, torsion of dermoid with endometrioma, or tubo-ovarian abscess.
6. **Dienogest is the medical mainstay for long-term pain management** – it is a fourth-generation progestin with high endometrial selectivity and low metabolic side effects. Cost limits use in some settings.
7. **Always consider co-existing adenomyosis** – heavy bleeding with pain suggests adenomyosis; endometriosis alone often has severe pain but normal flow. MRI or TVUS pre-treatment helps.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Sending patient home with NSAIDs and no referral → delays diagnosis by years
- Performing laparoscopy and not biopsying suspicious lesions → missed diagnosis
- Assuming negative laparoscopy means no endometriosis (false negative up to 20%)
- Starting GnRH agonist without add-back for >6 months → irreversible bone loss
- Recommending hysterectomy alone (if ovaries left) → recurrence risk high (ovarian hormone still stimulates remaining implants)
- Treating infertility with ART without first surgically treating large endometriomas → risk of infection/rupture during stimulation

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior/on-call team:**

| Scenario | Threshold / Signs | Action |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Severe acute pain unresponsive to oral analgesics** | VAS ≥8, unable to tolerate oral, need IV analgesia | Call gynecology registrar – consider laparoscopy for suspected rupture/torsion |
| **Peritoneal signs** | Rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds | Nil by mouth, IV fluids, urgent surgical evaluation (ruptured endometrioma, bowel perforation from DIE, torsion) |
| **Sepsis suspected** | Fever >38°C, tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate | NEWS2 ≥5, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, urgent imaging – possible tubo-ovarian abscess |
| **Hemodynamic instability** | SBP <90, HR >120, lactate >2 mmol/L | IV fluids, cross-match, call OBGYN reg ± general surgery reg (hemorrhage from ruptured cyst) |
| **Reduced urine output / flank pain** | Anuria, oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/hr, flank tenderness | Urgent renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis), urology referral |
| **GCS drop / seizure / severe headache** | Epidural or intracranial manifestation – rare (catamenial) | Call ICU, neurology – image brain for subarachnoid hemorrhage or epilepsy |
| **Suspected malignant transformation** | Solid component in endometrioma, rising CA-125 >200, family history of BRCA, postmenopausal | Gynaecologic oncology consult, MRI with contrast, tumor markers |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** triggers urgent clinical review
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with shock → ICU consult
- **GCS ≤14** in a patient on GnRH or hormonal therapy – rule out stroke/intracranial involvement
- **Serum sodium <125 mmol/L** if on GnRH agonist with mania/hyponatremia (SIADH-like effect reported rarely)

**Bottom line:** Endometriosis is a complex, chronic disease that requires a high index of suspicion, careful clinical exam, and a multidisciplinary approach. Your job as an FY1 is to recognize the pattern, start hormonal suppression early, and escalate promptly for acute complications. Always ask about pain with periods, deep pain during sex, and fertility goals. You can change a patient’s trajectory by diagnosing early.
